data.title: Copper Rises as Trump Signals Potential China Deal
data.description:
Copper futures rose above $4.57 per pound on Thursday, ending a three-day losing streak, as US President Donald Trump suggested that a new trade deal with China, the world’s top copper consumer, could be possible.

Geopolitical concerns also supported metal prices, with European Union officials agreeing to ban Russian primary aluminum imports as part of a broader sanctions package.

Meanwhile, in China, authorities have implemented restrictions on copper smelting due to excess industry capacity.

This overproduction has led to higher copper imports and declining inventories, although smelting firms are facing challenges in maintaining profitability.

In the US, Federal Reserve officials noted in January that they would prefer to see more progress on inflation before cutting interest rates further, expressing concern about the potential impact of Trump’s tariffs.

data.title: Siemens Raises $1.5 Billion From Siemens Healthineers Stake Sale
data.description:
By Helena Smolak

Siemens will raise around 1.45 billion euros ($1.51 billion) from the sale of its 2% stake in Siemens Healthineers.

The German technology company said Wednesday evening that it sold 26.5 million ordinary shares of the medical-technology maker at a price of 54.65 euros a share, representing a 2.9% discount to its closing price on Wednesday of 56.28 euros.

Its net proceeds from the sale, expected to close on Feb. 4, will fund general corporate purposes.

The shares were sold exclusively to qualified investors in a private placement through an accelerated bookbuilding process, it said.

Following the transactions, Siemens agreed to not sell shares for 90 days as part of a customary lock-up agreement. It currently holds a 73% stake in Siemens Healthineers.

The move comes after Siemens said in December that it would consider to reduce its majority stake in Siemens Healthineers, which it had listed in 2018, by around 5%.

Write to Helena Smolak at helena.smolak@wsj.com

data.title: The Hour Glass Repurchases More Shares
data.description:
The Hour Glass bought back 70,200 shares in the open market on Wednesday for SG$110,417 or SG$1.56912 apiece, according to a same-day filing with the Singapore Exchange.

The luxury watch retail group has been authorized to buy back 64.8 million shares under its current mandate. To date, it has bought back 591,000 shares.

data.title: Safestore's Fiscal Q1 Revenue Up
data.description:
Safestore Holdings said Thursday revenue increased year over year for the fiscal first quarter ended Jan. 31.

The self-storage company's revenue was 56.7 million pounds sterling, compared with 55.3 million pounds previously.

data.title: Pilbara Minerals Doesn't See U.S. Driving Lithium Recovery Off Course — Interview
data.description:
(07:19 GMT) *Pilbara Minerals CEO: Trump Policies May Slow, but Not Stop, U.S. EV Adoption
(07:19 GMT) *Pilbara Minerals CEO: U.S. Tariff Plans No Cause for Concern at This Point
(07:19 GMT) *Pilbara Minerals CEO: 'More Positive Than Ever' on Lithium Market Outlook
(07:19 GMT) *Pilbara Minerals CEO: Confident Pilgangoora Well Placed to Compete With Emerging DLE Technologies
(07:19 GMT) *Pilbara Minerals CEO: Current Lithium Prices Not Sustainable
(07:19 GMT) *Pilbara Minerals CEO: Can Weather Prolonged Lithium Market Rout, Highlights Cash Balance
(07:19 GMT) Pilbara Minerals Doesn't See U.S. Driving Lithium Recovery Off Course — Interview

By Rhiannon Hoyle

Demand for lithium-powered electric vehicles by American drivers could be slowed by President Trump's policy plans, said the chief executive of Pilbara Minerals, one of Australia's top lithium producers.

However, Dale Henderson said he isn't concerned that changes by the new U.S. administration will hold up any recovery of the global lithium market, given China's dominance in battery and EV production.

Trump's policies, including his aggressive plans for tariffs, are, "from my perspective, no cause for concern at this point," Henderson said in an interview Thursday following the company's first-half results.

"Yes, it's an important future market," but it is "outside of the U.S. that really speaks to the vast majority of today's lithium demand," Henderson said. Pilbara Minerals sells most of its lithium to China, although it intends to ramp up shipments to South Korea via a joint venture with materials company Posco.

The number of EVs and plug-in hybrids sold in China is now approaching the size of the entire car market in the U.S., according to Wards Intelligence.

Trump has threatened to withdraw tens of billions of dollars in subsidies for electric vehicles, which U.S. car executives say is crucial to get their wobbly EV operations profitable. The new administration has also halted federal funding for new EV charging stations, intended to help U.S. drivers overcome so-called range anxiety.

"I think the proposed changes that the Trump administration is considering, ultimately, that flows through to a slow rate of change for the U.S. market," said Henderson. "But I don't think it stops this transition."

Lithium prices, which have fallen sharply over the past two years, have recently shown signs of having bottomed, according to some investors and analysts. Yet a return to rising prices will likely take a pickup in demand or more supply cuts from producers, they said.

Henderson said he is "more positive than ever" about the outlook for the lithium market. "And the reason for that is evidence of strong underlying demand is there in spades," he said. "Outside the U.S. market, we're seeing other markets really picking up the pace."

Global lithium demand rose by nearly a third last year, fueled by higher EV sales and a sharp rise in demand for large-scale batteries to back up power grids, he said.

As lithium prices sank in recent years, Pilbara Minerals doubled down, expanding its Australian mine and buying a rival.

The company on Thursday said it swung to a net loss in the six months ended Dec. 31, as a sharp fall in prices for the lithium-rich spodumene concentrate it produces more than offset higher output.

"Across the industry, we're deep into the cost curve at this point, and not aware of any groups within the supply chain who are profitable or doing well," said Henderson. That suggests current prices aren't sustainable, he said.

Pilbara Minerals in October said it would suspend its Ngungaju processing plant in response to weak prices. But Henderson signaled he has no intention for further cutbacks.

"We've got A$1.2 billion (US$761 million) cash as at the end of the December quarter," he said. "That ensures that, yeah, we can weather the storm."

The company is ramping up production from its Pilgangoora lithium operation in Western Australia following a major expansion. Pilgangoora is one of the largest hard-rock lithium deposits in the world.

Pilbara Minerals reported a 28% rise in first-half production, while the price it fetched for sales fell by almost 60%.

Write to Rhiannon Hoyle at rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com

Arbuthnot Banking Expects Profit In Line With Market Views
data.description:
By Michael Susin

Arbuthnot Banking said it expects to report pretax profit for 2024 in line with market consensus after a positive performance in the fourth quarter.

The London-listed private and commercial bank said Thursday that it anticipates pretax profit for the year to be in line with the company-provided market consensus of 34.5 million pounds ($43.4 million), representing a drop from the 47.1 million pounds reported in 2023.

Write to Michael Susin at michael.susin@wsj.com

data.title: Mineral Resources' Fiscal H1 Results Weak, Jarden Research Says
data.description:
Mineral Resources' plunge in shares following its weak and disappointing fiscal H1 results, production guidance downgrades, and stronger-than-expected capital expenditure guidance is "rational," according to a Thursday filing with the Australian bourse.

The company reported Tuesday a fiscal first half-year loss of AU$4.1042 per diluted share, compared with earnings of AU$2.7844 per diluted share a year earlier.

Revenue for the six months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was AU$2.29 billion, down 9% from AU$2.52 billion in the same period a year earlier.

The mining firm lowered its fiscal year attributable volume guidance at its Onslow iron project between 8.8 million tonnes (mt) to 9.3 mt from its previous guidance of 10.5 mt to 11.7 mt.

Mineral Resources also lowered its mining services-related tonne production volumes to a range of 280 mt to 300 mt from its previous guidance of 295 mt to 315 mt.

Jarden believes MIN's balance sheet displays "signs of short-term stress" and is highly leveraged as the company's current ratio has dropped to 1.19 from 1.55 and highlights its slim margin of safety.

The investment firm questioned whether the company's AU$800 million revolver, crucial for liquidity, can be drawn due to undisclosed financial maintenance covenants.

The investment firm maintained the company's sell rating and price target of AU$20.

Shares of the company rose 7% at market close.

data.title: SAP Raises 2024 Dividend
data.description:
(07:02 GMT) *SAP Declares Dividend of EUR2.35
(07:03 GMT) *SAP Raises Dividend to EUR2.35 Vs EUR2.20 a Share
(07:13 GMT) SAP Raises 2024 Dividend

By Helena Smolak

SAP raised its full-year dividend for 2024 to 2.35 euros ($2.45) a share from 2.20 euros a share the year before.

The European multinational software company said Thursday that the distributed amount in dividends would total approximately 2.74 billion euros, if approved by shareholders.

Write to Helena Smolak at helena.smolak@wsj.com

data.title: Prospects of Higher Debt Issuance in Eurozone Drives Bond Yields Higher — Market Talk
data.description: Expectations for higher debt supply due to defense spending keep eurozone government bonds under pressure, says Danske Bank Research's August Hyldgaard in a note. "Expectations for higher debt issuance and a string of better-than-expected data, and less geopolitical turmoil may all be attributable to this," the assistant analyst says. Eurozone bonds were sold Wednesday, pushing 10-year bond yields higher by up to 9 bps, according to Tradeweb. The 10-year Bund yield closed at 2.55% on Wednesday, the highest level since the end of January, according to Tradeweb data. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

Waaree Energies Secures 362.5 MWp Solar Module Order from Engie Unit
data.description:
Waaree Energies said it has received an order to supply 362.5-megawatt-peak of solar photovoltaic modules to Khaba Renewable Energy, a subsidiary of Engie India, according to an Indian bourse filing on Wednesday.

The supply is set to begin in the fiscal year starting April 1, the filing said.

data.title: Swiss Trade Surplus Smallest in 4 Months
data.description:
Switzerland’s trade surplus narrowed to CHF 4.0 billion in January 2025, down from an upwardly revised CHF 4.4 billion in December, marking the smallest surplus in four periods.

Imports declined 6.8% month-on-month to CHF 18.7 billion, driven by decreased purchases of chemical and pharmaceutical products (-19.5%), vehicles (-5%), machine and electronics (-2.1%).

By country, imports notably fell from Australia (-57.5%), Japan (-34%), and Canada (-31.9%).

Meanwhile, exports dropped at slightly faster 6.9% to CHF 22.7 billion, reflecting lower sales of chemical and pharmaceutical products (-12.5%), paper and graphic products (-7.3%), precision instruments (-0.4%), and vehicles (-0.3%).

Among trading partners, demand went down mainly to Slovenia (-35.1%), China (-30%), and Austria (-22.8%).
